questions.question: A student has $40 to spend on notebooks (x) and pens (y). Notebooks cost $5 each and pens cost $2 each. The student needs at least 3 notebooks and at least 4 pens. Which system of inequalities models this situation?
questions.explanation: The budget constraint limits total spending: 5x + 2y ≤ 40. 'At least 3 notebooks' means x ≥ 3. 'At least 4 pens' means y ≥ 4. Option A incorrectly uses ≥ for the budget. Options B and D reverse the minimum constraints.

questions.question: A club sells tickets: adult tickets (x) for $10 and child tickets (y) for $6. They must sell at least 50 tickets total and earn at least $400. Which system models this?
questions.explanation: 'At least 50 tickets' translates to x + y ≥ 50. 'Earn at least $400' translates to 10x + 6y ≥ 400. Both are minimum requirements, so both use ≥. Options B, C, and D have at least one inequality direction wrong.

questions.question: A manufacturer makes chairs (x) and tables (y). Each chair requires 2 hours of labor; each table requires 5 hours. The factory has at most 40 labor hours available. Chairs require 3 units of wood; tables require 4 units. At most 36 units of wood are available. Which system models these constraints?
questions.explanation: 'At most' means ≤ for both constraints. Labor: 2 hours per chair and 5 per table, total ≤ 40. Wood: 3 units per chair and 4 per table, total ≤ 36. Option A reverses both inequalities. Option C swaps the coefficients. Option D has one inequality reversed.

questions.question: A dietician recommends a meal with at least 60 grams of protein and at most 800 calories. Chicken (x oz) has 20g protein and 160 calories per ounce. Rice (y oz) has 5g protein and 100 calories per ounce. Which system models these constraints?
questions.explanation: 'At least 60g protein' → 20x + 5y ≥ 60. 'At most 800 calories' → 160x + 100y ≤ 800. Option B reverses both. Option C has calories as a minimum. Option D has protein as a maximum.

questions.question: A pet store sells dogs (x) and cats (y). They can house at most 30 animals total. They need at least twice as many cats as dogs. Which point represents a feasible combination?
questions.explanation: (8, 20) satisfies total animals ≤ 30 (28 ≤ 30) and cats ≥ twice dogs (20 ≥ 16). (12, 18) fails the ratio (18 ≥ 24, false). (10, 15) fails the ratio (15 ≥ 20, false). (15, 10) fails the ratio (10 ≥ 30, false).

questions.question: A gardener plants flowers (x) and shrubs (y). Each flower needs 3 sq ft of space; each shrub needs 8 sq ft. The garden is at most 120 sq ft. The gardener wants at least 5 flowers and at least 4 shrubs. Which point is NOT feasible?
questions.explanation: (30, 5) uses 3(30) + 8(5) = 90 + 40 = 130 sq ft, exceeding the 120 sq ft garden limit. (10, 8), (8, 10), and (20, 6) all satisfy the space constraint and the minimum requirements.

questions.question: A company produces two products: A (x units) and B (y units). Product A requires 1 hour of machine time and 3 hours of assembly. Product B requires 2 hours of machine time and 2 hours of assembly. Machine time is limited to 16 hours; assembly is limited to 24 hours. Which system models the constraints?
questions.explanation: Machine hours: 1 per A + 2 per B = x + 2y, limited to 16 → x + 2y ≤ 16. Assembly hours: 3 per A + 2 per B = 3x + 2y, limited to 24 → 3x + 2y ≤ 24. Option B incorrectly uses ≥. Options C and D swap the coefficients.

questions.question: A party planner orders small centerpieces (x) at $15 each and large centerpieces (y) at $25 each. The budget is at most $300. The number of large centerpieces must be at least half the number of small ones. Which system models this?
questions.explanation: Budget 'at most $300' → 15x + 25y ≤ 300. Large (y) 'at least half the number of small ones (x)' → y ≥ 0.5x. Option A has y ≥ 2x (large at least twice small). Option C reverses the budget inequality. Option D reverses the ratio.

questions.question: A fundraiser sells bracelets (x) for $4 and necklaces (y) for $12. The goal is to sell at most 50 items total and raise at least $360. Which point meets both goals?

A concert venue has floor seats (x) at $80 each and balcony seats (y) at $50 each. Capacity is at most 500 seats. To break even, revenue must be at least $32,000. Which point represents a feasible combination?

scoreMessages.90: Excellent work! You can accurately model real-world situations with systems of linear inequalities and verify feasible solutions.
scoreMessages.70: Strong skills! Focus on: 1) Translating 'at most' to ≤ and 'at least' to ≥, 2) Identifying the correct coefficients for each variable from the problem context, 3) Testing points in all constraints to verify feasibility.
scoreMessages.50: Good progress. Remember: Read word problems carefully to identify which quantity is limited from above (≤) and which is a minimum requirement (≥). Set up each constraint separately before combining into a system. Always include non-negative constraints when dealing with real quantities.
scoreMessages.0: Start by: 1) Define your variables clearly, 2) Identify each constraint and translate key phrases: 'at most' → ≤, 'at least' → ≥, 'no more than' → ≤, 'minimum' → ≥, 3) Write the inequality for each constraint with the correct coefficients, 4) Test points by substituting into ALL inequalities of the system.
